input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a teleoperated, 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. It consists of a lower and an upper chassis that integrate autonomy elements (including the ACC control unit). Operating in a test‑track environment, the twin streams real‑time ACC‑relevant data and serves as a realistic, reconfigurable platform for assessing computation offloading. Its compact size, on‑board sensor suite, and modular design make it well suited for detailed digital twinning and performance monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin System (DTS) delivers three main services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface that visualises real‑time vehicle state, projected trajectory and nearby object tracks; it also permits viewing, saving and report generation from collected telemetry. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC decision logic is executed remotely (e.g., on a cloud server) to relieve on‑board ECU load; the twin then transmits the computed desired speed back to the vehicle actuators. (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speeds, or issue emergency braking commands through the UI; all actions are transmitted over a low end‑to‑end latency link. These services cover monitoring, visualization, remote control and compute optimisation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a low‑latency vehicle‑to‑twin link (e.g., 5 G/Wi‑Fi/DSRC), cloud computing resources for the offloaded ACC algorithm, and a telemetry‑collection infrastructure that streams sensor and state data to the twin. The Digital Twin System bundles the twin entity, physical twin, middleware components and UI, with adherence to safety‑critical requirements. While the document does not name specific software stacks, the twin employs standard data‑collection and reporting databases, real‑time streaming protocols, and a UI enabling monitoring, reporting and remote command execution.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin synchronizes its internal state with the physical vehicle using a shared time base that achieves 100 ms absolute accuracy, as constrained by safety‑critical vehicular standards. Computation offloading between on‑board units depends on this coarse time alignment, enabling coordinated sensor fusion, vehicle‑to‑vehicle communication, and actuator command scheduling. All ROS2 nodes within the twin use the standard ROS time source, ensuring that timestamps on published messages and logged data are consistent across the distributed deployment.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: A single Digital Twin instance is deployed per vehicle, comprised of three primary functional entities—User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin—each instantiated on distinct hardware nodes. The User Entity hosts a split User Interface Functional Element, represented by a separate 3D visualization package and a control‑panel package, while the Digital Twin Entity contains data‑management and control‑mechanism packages, and the Physical Twin Entity handles pre‑processing, actuation, and device communication functions. This distribution results in a decentralized, component‑based deployment without multiple twin replicas.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture, mapping each Functional Element (FE) onto a ROS2 package that contains one or more ROS2 nodes. The system adopts an entity‑based structure: User Entity (visualization, control panel), Digital Twin Entity (data storage, control logic), and Physical Twin Entity (sensor/actuator handling, point‑cloud clustering, tracking). Resources such as compute nodes, sensors, and actuators are allocated per entity, enabling distributed processing across a vehicle’s network. ROS2 topics, services, and actions provide the intra‑system coordination mechanism, while the underlying middleware ensures message ordering, QoS, and fault tolerance. This constellation deviates from ISO 23247 by introducing a Physical Twin Entity that integrates general automotive functionality directly into the twin’s core resources.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the vehicle’s physical twin – including LiDAR point clouds, on‑board camera frames, and vehicle state telemetry – is captured by the Physical Twin Entity and immediately forwarded to the Digital Twin’s Data Translation component.  The Data Translation FE serializes each stream, aligns it with the ROS 2 message types used by the Digital Twin, and performs unit conversions to ensure consistency across subsystems.  Pre‑processing is carried out by the Point‑Cloud Clustering & Tracking SW component, which transforms raw 3‑D point clouds into 2‑D plane points, identifies object tracks, and projects driving paths for downstream monitoring.  Communication between software components flows via a ROS 2 middleware (DDS) over a UDP multicast bus, while inter‑entity data exchange (e.g., between the Physical Twin and the Digital Twin) occurs over the vehicle’s Wi‑Fi network using UDP sockets.  Data quality is maintained through timestamping, GPS alignment, and the Data Translation step, which also verifies payload integrity and performs type validation before integration into the 3‑D visualization and reporting layers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin System’s internal communication relies on ROS 2, which uses the Data Distribution Service (DDS) over UDP multicast for low‑latency, high‑throughput message distribution.  Inter‑entity communication is carried out over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer, as described in the architecture diagram.  The documentation does not provide explicit bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security requirements for the network infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliant with ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing, adopting its reference architecture. The Digital Twin Entity is implemented per ISO 23247 Part 2 using an OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) stack for interoperability, ensuring adherence to the standard’s requirements for data structure, security, and communication protocols. The system references ISO 23247 Part 3 for digital representation of manufacturing elements and Part 4 for information exchange, thereby aligning with the specification for data exchange and model representation. The publicly available implementation on GitHub (https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin) follows the ISO 23247 guidance, demonstrating compliance with industry interoperability standards and providing a foundation for potential certification in OEM manufacturing environments.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: ISO 26262‑1:2018 mandates a formal hazard analysis and functional safety concept that requires the identification of safety goals, the application of FMEA and fault‑tree analyses, and the definition of fail‑safe mechanisms such as redundancy, graceful degradation and deterministic reaction times. The standard also prescribes a safety lifecycle covering requirements, design, implementation, verification and validation, and defines a safety case that must be maintained throughout a vehicle’s operational life. 3GPP TS 22.185 for V2X services specifies that all V2X messages must be sent with protection of confidentiality, integrity, authentication and non‑repudiation. The architecture relies on a V2X Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) with certificates to authenticate nodes, AES‑128/256 encryption to ensure confidentiality, and hash‑based message authentication codes or digital signatures to guarantee integrity. Closely related is the use of access‑control lists and role‑based privileges on the UDS and 5G core network, along with intrusion‑detection and DDoS mitigation mechanisms. Through these measures, the system achieves a multi‑layered defense that addresses both cybersecurity threats and functional safety risks.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_
block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
